expanded_description: Linear equations and inequalities form the foundation of algebraic problem-solving in Pre-calculus. From basic one-variable equations to compound inequalities and absolute value problems, mastering linear relationships enables students to model real-world situations, analyze functions, solve systems, and prepare for calculus concepts like linear approximation and optimization. Understanding solution sets, interval notation, and inequality properties is essential for success in advanced mathematics.

core_concept.principle:
A linear equation in one variable can be written as $ax + b = c$ or $ax + b = cx + d$, where $a \neq 0$. Solving involves isolating the variable using inverse operations while maintaining equality. A linear inequality ($<$, $>$, $\leq$, $\geq$) follows similar steps, but multiplying or dividing by a negative number reverses the inequality symbol.

Equation Solutions:
• One solution: $x = \text{constant}$
• No solution: contradictory statement (e.g., $0 = 5$)
• Infinite solutions: identity (e.g., $0 = 0$ or $x = x$)

Inequality Solutions:
• Solution sets are intervals or unions of intervals
• Graph on number line with open/closed circles
• Express in interval notation or set-builder notation

Absolute Value Equations/Inequalities:
• $|X| = k$ → $X = k$ or $X = -k$ (for $k \geq 0$)
• $|X| < k$ → $-k < X < k$ (and inequality)
• $|X| > k$ → $X < -k$ or $X > k$ (or inequality)

Properties of Inequality:
• Adding/subtracting same quantity preserves inequality
• Multiplying/dividing by positive number preserves inequality
• Multiplying/dividing by negative number reverses inequality
core_concept.explanation: Think of equations as balanced scales—whatever you do to one side must be done to the other to maintain balance. Inequalities are similar, but with a critical twist: multiplying or dividing by a negative flips the inequality direction (like a see-saw tipping the opposite way). In Pre-calculus, these skills extend to solving linear systems, polynomial inequalities, rational inequalities, and absolute value equations. Understanding interval notation and solution set representation is essential for communicating answers clearly and preparing for domain and range analysis of functions.

image.alt: Overview of solving linear equations and inequalities
image.caption: Figure 1.5.1: Linear equations and inequalities—foundation of algebra.

sections.section_title: 1.5.1 Solving Linear Equations in One Variable
sections.introduction: Linear equations in one variable have the form $ax + b = c$ or $ax + b = cx + d$, where $a$ and $c$ are coefficients and $b$, $d$ are constants.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 1.5.2: Solving linear equations by isolating the variable.

key_concept.definition:
General Strategy:
1. Clear fractions (multiply by LCD) if present
2. Distribute to remove parentheses
3. Combine like terms on each side
4. Collect variable terms on one side, constants on the other
5. Isolate variable using inverse operations
6. Check solution in original equation

Special Cases:
• Conditional Equation: One solution ($a \neq 0$)
• Contradiction: No solution (e.g., $0 = 5$)
• Identity: Infinite solutions (e.g., $0 = 0$ or $x = x$)
key_concept.context: Linear equations model countless real-world situations: distance-rate-time, cost-profit, temperature conversion, and more. In Pre-calculus, solving linear equations is prerequisite for solving systems, finding inverses, working with linear functions, and understanding linear approximation in calculus.

image.caption: Figure 1.5.3: Step-by-step strategy for linear equations.

worked_examples.title: Basic Linear Equation

image.caption: Figure 1.5.4: Isolating the variable.

worked_examples.problem: Solve $3x - 7 = 2x + 5$.

step_by_step.step: Collect variable terms on one side

worked_examples.concept_applied: Collect variable terms, then isolate using inverse operations.

worked_examples.title: Equation with Fractions

image.caption: Figure 1.5.5: Clearing fractions by multiplying by LCD.

worked_examples.problem: Solve $\frac{2x}{3} - \frac{x-1}{2} = \frac{5}{6}$.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Multiply by LCD to clear fractions, then solve linear equation.

worked_examples.title: Special Case: No Solution

image.caption: Figure 1.5.6: Equation with no solution.

worked_examples.problem: Solve $3x - 5 = 3x + 2$.

step_by_step.calculation: No value of $x$ makes this true

worked_examples.concept_applied: Contradiction means equation has no solution; variable terms cancel leaving false statement.

worked_examples.title: Special Case: Infinite Solutions

image.caption: Figure 1.5.7: Equation with infinite solutions.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Identity means equation holds for all $x$; variable terms cancel leaving true statement.

image.caption: Figure 1.5.8: Solving linear equations—summary of cases.

sections.section_title: 1.5.2 Solving Linear Inequalities in One Variable
sections.introduction: Linear inequalities ($<$, $>$, $\leq$, $\geq$) are solved similarly to equations, but multiplying or dividing by a negative number reverses the inequality symbol.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 1.5.9: Inequality symbols and number line graphs.

key_concept.definition:
Properties of Inequalities:
• If $a < b$, then $a + c < b + c$ (addition property)
• If $a < b$ and $c > 0$, then $ac < bc$ (multiplication by positive)
• If $a < b$ and $c < 0$, then $ac > bc$ (multiplication by negative reverses sign)

Solution Representation:
• Set-builder notation: $\{x \mid x > 3\}$
• Interval notation: $(3, \infty)$ for $x > 3$; $[3, \infty)$ for $x \geq 3$
• Number line graph: Open circle for $<$ or $>$; closed circle for $\leq$ or $\geq$

Compound Inequalities:
• And: $x > 2$ and $x < 5$ → $2 < x < 5$ → $(2,5)$
• Or: $x < 2$ or $x > 5$ → $(-\infty, 2) \cup (5, \infty)$
key_concept.context: Linear inequalities model real-world constraints: minimum/maximum values, budgets, thresholds, and ranges. In Pre-calculus, inequality skills are essential for domain/range analysis, solving polynomial/rational inequalities, absolute value inequalities, and optimization problems.

image.caption: Figure 1.5.10: Solving inequalities—remember to flip sign when multiplying/dividing by negative.

worked_examples.title: Basic Linear Inequality

image.caption: Figure 1.5.11: Solving and graphing linear inequalities.

worked_examples.problem: Solve $4x - 7 \geq 2x + 5$, graph solution, and write in interval notation.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Solve like equation; inequality direction unchanged when dividing by positive number.

worked_examples.title: Inequality with Negative Multiplication

image.caption: Figure 1.5.12: Reversing inequality when multiplying/dividing by negative.

worked_examples.problem: Solve $-3x + 5 < 14$, graph, and write in interval notation.

step_by_step.explanation: Divide both sides by $-3$ (negative!) — FLIP inequality symbol

worked_examples.concept_applied: Multiplying or dividing by a negative number reverses the inequality direction.

worked_examples.title: Compound Inequality (And)

image.caption: Figure 1.5.13: Solving 'and' compound inequalities.

worked_examples.problem: Solve $-5 < 2x + 3 \leq 7$ and write in interval notation.

step_by_step.explanation: Divide all parts by $2$ (positive, signs unchanged)

worked_examples.concept_applied: Compound 'and' inequality can be solved by isolating variable in the middle; apply operations to all three parts.

worked_examples.title: Compound Inequality (Or)

image.caption: Figure 1.5.14: Solving 'or' compound inequalities.

worked_examples.problem: Solve $2x - 3 \leq 5$ or $3x + 1 > 10$, and write in interval notation.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Compound 'or' inequality takes union of individual solution sets.

image.caption: Figure 1.5.15: Solving linear inequalities—summary of rules and interval notation.

sections.introduction: Absolute value equations involve the distance from zero. Solving requires considering both positive and negative cases.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 1.5.16: Absolute value as distance from zero.

key_concept.definition:
Definition: $|a| = \begin{cases} a & \text{if } a \geq 0 \\ -a & \text{if } a < 0 \end{cases}$

Solving $|X| = k$ (where $k \geq 0$):
$|X| = k$ → $X = k$ or $X = -k$

Important Notes:
• If $k < 0$, equation has no solution (absolute value cannot be negative)
• Isolate absolute value expression before splitting into cases
• Check solutions in original equation (both may be valid)
key_concept.context: Absolute value equations model distance problems, tolerance ranges, and error analysis. In Pre-calculus, absolute value concepts extend to functions, transformations, and complex numbers.

image.caption: Figure 1.5.17: Solving $|X| = k$ by considering positive and negative cases.

worked_examples.title: Basic Absolute Value Equation

image.caption: Figure 1.5.18: Solving $|x - 3| = 5$.

worked_examples.problem: Solve $|x - 3| = 5$.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Absolute value equation $|X| = k$ (with $k > 0$) yields two equations: $X = k$ and $X = -k$.

worked_examples.title: Isolate Absolute Value First

image.caption: Figure 1.5.19: Isolate absolute value expression before solving.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Always isolate absolute value before splitting into cases.

worked_examples.title: Absolute Value Equation with No Solution

image.caption: Figure 1.5.20: Absolute value cannot equal negative number.

worked_examples.concept_applied: $|X| = k$ has no solution when $k < 0$.

image.caption: Figure 1.5.21: Solving absolute value equations.

sections.introduction: Absolute value inequalities involve distance comparisons. The pattern depends on whether the inequality is 'less than' or 'greater than'.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 1.5.22: Absolute value inequalities as distance from zero.

key_concept.definition:
For $k > 0$:
• $|X| < k$ → $-k < X < k$ (and inequality—inside interval)
• $|X| \leq k$ → $-k \leq X \leq k$
• $|X| > k$ → $X < -k$ or $X > k$ (or inequality—outside interval)
• $|X| \geq k$ → $X \leq -k$ or $X \geq k$

For $k \leq 0$:
• $|X| < k$: No solution if $k \leq 0$ (except possibly $|X| < 0$ impossible)
• $|X| > k$: All real numbers if $k < 0$ (absolute value always $\geq 0 > k$)

Strategy:
1. Isolate absolute value expression
2. Apply appropriate pattern based on inequality symbol
3. Solve resulting compound inequality
4. Express solution in interval notation
key_concept.context: Absolute value inequalities model tolerance ranges, error bounds, and real-world constraints like 'within 2 units of 5'. In Pre-calculus, these skills extend to complex numbers, function transformations, and epsilon-delta proofs in calculus.

image.caption: Figure 1.5.23: Patterns for $|X| < k$ (inside) and $|X| > k$ (outside).

worked_examples.title: Less Than Inequality (And Pattern)

image.caption: Figure 1.5.24: $|X| < k$ represents values within $k$ units of zero.

worked_examples.problem: Solve $|2x - 1| \leq 5$ and write in interval notation.

step_by_step.explanation: Divide all parts by 2 (positive, signs unchanged)

worked_examples.concept_applied: $|X| \leq k$ becomes compound inequality $-k \leq X \leq k$.

worked_examples.title: Greater Than Inequality (Or Pattern)

image.caption: Figure 1.5.25: $|X| > k$ represents values more than $k$ units from zero.

worked_examples.problem: Solve $|3x + 2| > 7$ and write in interval notation.
worked_examples.concept_applied: $|X| > k$ becomes compound inequality $X < -k$ or $X > k$.

worked_examples.title: Isolate Absolute Value First

image.caption: Figure 1.5.26: Isolate absolute value expression first.

worked_examples.problem: Solve $2|4x - 3| + 1 \leq 9$.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Always isolate absolute value before applying inequality patterns.

image.caption: Figure 1.5.27: Solving absolute value inequalities.

sections.section_title: 1.5.5 Applications of Linear Equations and Inequalities
sections.introduction: Linear equations and inequalities model real-world situations involving rates, costs, tolerances, and constraints.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 1.5.28: Applying linear equations and inequalities to real problems.

key_concept.definition:
Common Application Types:
• Distance = Rate × Time: $d = rt$
• Cost/Revenue/Profit: $C = mx + b$, $R = px$, $P = R - C$
• Mixture Problems: Combine quantities with different concentrations
• Tolerance/Error Bounds: $|x - target| \leq tolerance$
• Temperature Conversion: $F = \frac{9}{5}C + 32$
• Geometry Problems: Perimeter, area relationships
key_concept.context: Linear models appear throughout Pre-calculus and calculus in contexts like optimization, related rates, and linear approximation. Understanding how to translate word problems into equations and inequalities is essential for applied mathematics.

image.caption: Figure 1.5.29: Common linear application formulas.

worked_examples.title: Distance-Rate-Time Problem

image.caption: Figure 1.5.30: Using $d = rt$ to solve travel problems.

worked_examples.problem: Two cars start from the same point and travel in opposite directions. One travels at 55 mph, the other at 65 mph. How long until they are 360 miles apart?
worked_examples.concept_applied: For opposite directions, total distance = sum of individual distances.

worked_examples.title: Profit Inequality

image.caption: Figure 1.5.31: Using inequalities to find profit conditions.

worked_examples.problem: A company produces widgets at a cost of $5 per widget plus fixed costs of $2000. They sell each widget for $12. How many widgets must they sell to make a profit of at least $3000?

step_by_step.calculation: Must sell at least 715 widgets (since whole widgets)

worked_examples.concept_applied: Profit = Revenue - Cost; inequality models minimum profit requirement.

worked_examples.title: Tolerance/Absolute Value Application

image.caption: Figure 1.5.32: Using absolute value to model acceptable tolerances.

worked_examples.problem: A machine fills bottles with 500 mL of water. The acceptable tolerance is ±5 mL. Write an absolute value inequality for acceptable volumes and solve.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Absolute value inequalities model tolerance: $|x - target| \leq tolerance$.

worked_examples.title: Mixture Problem

image.caption: Figure 1.5.33: Mixing solutions to achieve target concentration.

worked_examples.problem: How many liters of a 20% acid solution must be added to 10 liters of a 50% acid solution to obtain a solution that is at most 40% acid?
worked_examples.concept_applied: Mixture problems use weighted averages; inequality models concentration constraint.

image.caption: Figure 1.5.34: Linear equation and inequality applications summary.

sections.section_title: 1.5.6 Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: Linear equations and inequalities involve common pitfalls. Recognizing these errors builds algebraic precision and confidence.

classifications.value: Forgetting to reverse inequality when multiplying or dividing by negative.
characteristics: Not flipping sign when dividing by negative coefficient
Multiplying both sides of inequality by variable without knowing sign
classifications.behavior: Results in incorrect solution set (often opposite direction).

image.caption: Figure 1.5.35: Error - forgetting to reverse inequality with negative multiplication.

classifications.value: Mixing up 'less than' and 'greater than' patterns for absolute value.
characteristics: Writing $|X| < k$ as $X < -k$ or $X > k$ (incorrect—should be 'and')
Writing $|X| > k$ as $-k < X < k$ (incorrect—should be 'or')
Forgetting that $|X| < k$ has no solution when $k \leq 0$
classifications.behavior: Produces incorrect solution intervals (union vs intersection mismatch).

image.caption: Figure 1.5.36: Error - confusing 'and' vs 'or' patterns.

classifications.value: Failing to distribute negative sign to all terms when subtracting expressions.
classifications.behavior: Results in sign errors for one or more terms.

image.caption: Figure 1.5.37: Error - sign errors when subtracting expressions.

classifications.value: Incorrectly writing compound inequalities or misplacing endpoints.
characteristics: Using wrong inequality symbols on endpoints
Confusing union vs intersection in interval notation
classifications.behavior: Produces invalid or incomplete solution sets.

image.caption: Figure 1.5.38: Error - incorrect compound inequality representation.

classifications.value: Not checking solutions in absolute value equations (though less common than radical equations).
characteristics: Assuming both algebraic solutions always valid
classifications.behavior: Includes invalid solutions that don't satisfy original equation.

image.caption: Figure 1.5.39: Error - not checking solutions in absolute value equations.

examples.explanation: Correct: $|2x - 1| = -3$ has no solution because absolute value cannot be negative.

worked_examples.title: Identifying and Correcting Inequality Errors

image.caption: Figure 1.5.40: Finding and fixing inequality mistakes.

worked_examples.problem: A student solved $-2x + 5 > 11$ and got $x > -3$. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.calculation: Student divided by $-2$ but didn't flip inequality

worked_examples.final_answer: $x < -3$ (student forgot to reverse inequality when dividing by negative)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Always reverse inequality when multiplying or dividing by a negative number.

image.caption: Figure 1.5.41: Common errors in linear equations and inequalities.

sections.section_title: 1.5.7 Practice with Linear Equations and Inequalities
sections.introduction: Apply equation and inequality solving skills to a variety of problems.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Practice Set

image.caption: Figure 1.5.42: Practice with linear equations and inequalities.

worked_examples.problem:
Solve each equation, inequality, or application:

1) $5x - 3 = 2x + 12$
2) $\frac{2x}{3} - \frac{x-2}{4} = \frac{5}{6}$
3) $3(x - 4) = 3x - 12$ (classify)
4) $2x + 7 \leq 5x - 2$
5) $-4x + 3 > 15$
6) $-3 < 2x + 1 \leq 7$
7) $2x - 5 \leq 1$ or $3x + 2 > 11$
8) $|2x - 5| = 9$
9) $3|x + 2| - 4 = 11$
10) $|3x - 1| \leq 8$
11) $|4x + 3| > 5$
12) A car rental charges $30 per day plus $0.25 per mile. You have $100 budget. How many miles can you drive?
13) A machinist must produce parts within 0.05 cm of 12.5 cm. Write and solve absolute value inequality for acceptable lengths.
worked_examples.final_answer:
1) $x = 5$
2) $x = \frac{4}{5}$
3) All real numbers (identity)
4) $[3, \infty)$
5) $(-\infty, -3)$
6) $(-2, 3]$
7) All real numbers
8) $x = -2, 7$
9) $x = -7, 3$
10) $[-\frac{7}{3}, 3]$
11) $(-\infty, -2) \cup (\frac{1}{2}, \infty)$
12) At most 280 miles
13) $[12.45, 12.55]$ cm
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive practice with linear equations, inequalities, compound inequalities, absolute value equations/inequalities, and applications.

image.caption: Figure 1.5.43: Complete practice solutions.

summary: Linear equations and inequalities form the foundation of algebraic problem-solving. Linear equations in one variable ($ax + b = c$ or $ax + b = cx + d$) are solved by isolating the variable using inverse operations. Solutions may be unique (conditional equation), none (contradiction), or infinite (identity). Linear inequalities follow similar steps, but multiplying or dividing by a negative number reverses the inequality symbol. Solution sets are expressed in interval notation ($(a,b)$, $[a,b]$, $[a,b)$, $(a,\infty)$, etc.) or set-builder notation. Compound inequalities combine conditions with 'and' (intersection, $x$ between bounds) or 'or' (union, $x$ outside bounds). Absolute value equations $|X| = k$ (with $k \geq 0$) split into $X = k$ or $X = -k$; if $k < 0$, no solution. Absolute value inequalities follow patterns: $|X| < k$ becomes $-k < X < k$ (and/inside), while $|X| > k$ becomes $X < -k$ or $X > k$ (or/outside). Applications include distance-rate-time, profit analysis, tolerance ranges, mixture problems, and temperature conversion. Common errors include forgetting to reverse inequality direction when multiplying/dividing by negative, confusing absolute value patterns, sign errors in subtraction, and misrepresenting compound inequalities. Mastery of these skills is essential for Pre-calculus success, providing the foundation for solving systems, analyzing functions, and preparing for calculus concepts like linear approximation and epsilon-delta proofs.
